speaker: Jeannie
en: Hi Dan.

speaker: Jeannie
en: How's it going?

speaker: Dan
en: Hello Jeannie.

speaker: Dan
en: Pretty well, thanks.

speaker: Dan
en: Have you finished your research yet?

speaker: Jeannie
en: Yes, it's all done.

speaker: Jeannie
en: I've spent several weeks on it, from the very beginning of deciding the topic, to carrying out the research, and writing the essay.

speaker: Dan
en: Congratulations!

speaker: Dan
en: It seems like a big project.

speaker: Dan
en: I know the research is to do with music, but what is it exactly about?

speaker: Jeannie
en: Well, at first I didn't have a specific purpose until one day I read a report that said 'if appropriate music is played while people are eating, it may cause some changes in their behaviour'.

speaker: Jeannie
en: But the report didn't illustrate the changes in detail, so I wanted to carry out a thorough investigation into this.

speaker: Dan
en: That's quite unique.

speaker: Dan
en: So, how did you conduct your research?

speaker: Jeannie
en: I started by looking for five different restaurants, and then went to each of the restaurants to order some food and observe other people.

speaker: Jeannie
en: I mainly focused on how the music is played instead of how people eat.

speaker: Jeannie
en: Findings of my observations indicate that the five restaurants have something in common – they all have a special section to play the music.

speaker: Jeannie
en: More importantly, although the music is played at different times, the volume is unchanged.

speaker: Dan
en: Sounds attractive.

speaker: Dan
en: I've never read anything about this before.

speaker: Dan
en: In addition to observation, what other research methods have you used?

speaker: Jeannie
en: I also distributed the survey questionnaire to people dining there.

speaker: Dan
en: What questions are posed in your questionnaire?

speaker: Jeannie
en: Well, the first section of the questionnaire asks subjects to indicate the number of times they have been to the restaurant previously, the number of people they dine with and whether they think they will return to the restaurant.

speaker: Jeannie
en: These early questions don't involve musical preferences, but largely act as 'distracters' – the intention being that subjects would be less inclined to think that the survey is about the music being played.

speaker: Dan
en: That sounds very reasonable.

speaker: Dan
en: Did you design the questionnaire by yourself?

speaker: Jeannie
en: Actually no, I read an assignment a student wrote last year, which was just kept beside a dictionary in the library.

speaker: Jeannie
en: I came across it when I was looking something up in the dictionary.

speaker: Dan
en: Oh, you were so lucky.

speaker: Dan
en: Is there any other question included in the questionnaire?

speaker: Jeannie
en: Yes, subjects are also asked why they choose to dine at a particular restaurant.

speaker: Jeannie
en: Several reasons are listed in the questionnaire.

speaker: Jeannie
en: I thought people would put service first, but that's not the case.

speaker: Jeannie
en: Only about 12% of them have chosen this reason, while the majority of respondents think whether they will pick this restaurant depends on how many available seats it has.

speaker: Jeannie
en: They think it's terrible to eat in a crowded place.

speaker: Dan
en: Yes, everyone wants to enjoy a meal comfortably.

speaker: Dan
en: Then I guess maybe the location of a restaurant is an important factor since no one likes to waste too much time on the way?

speaker: Jeannie
en: It used to be, but nowadays nearly every household has a private car, so they don't have to be worried about the traffic problem.

speaker: Jeannie
en: What I find interesting, however, is that a large percentage of people say they will compare different restaurants in the neighbourhood on the Internet first.

speaker: Jeannie
en: If one of them stands out from its rivals, they will choose it.

speaker: Dan
en: Wow, it's really interesting.

speaker: Dan
en: What else then?

speaker: Jeannie
en: Well, the second section of the questionnaire asks subjects to respond to the music being played by rating so that the relationship between music and perceived atmosphere could be examined directly.

speaker: Jeannie
en: As a means of examining purchase intention, the final section of the questionnaire asks them to indicate the maximum amount of money they are prepared to spend on their main meal.

speaker: Dan
en: So what have you finally discovered?

speaker: Jeannie
en: I collected all the questionnaires and analysed people's answers to get a clear idea of their reactions to different music conditions.

speaker: Jeannie
en: The result shows that music has become an indispensable part of restaurants.

speaker: Jeannie
en: If people find there isn't any music during the meal, they will think the restaurant charges too much.

speaker: Dan
en: Yes, now music is everywhere in our life.

speaker: Dan
en: Is there any difference in people's attitudes towards different musical styles?

speaker: Jeannie
en: Yes.

speaker: Jeannie
en: Most people think that if jazz is being played when eating, they tend to stay at the restaurant longer and order more food to enjoy the music.

speaker: Jeannie
en: But this is quite the opposite of what happens when classical music is played.

speaker: Jeannie
en: Many customers think if the restaurant plays classical music, they wouldn't like to stay there for another minute, but to finish the food quickly and hurry away, because they don't like this type of music at all.

speaker: Jeannie
en: And lastly, about pop music, it's very popular these days, especially among young people, as I've noticed in restaurants.

speaker: Jeannie
en: They like listening to this type of music during their meals.

speaker: Jeannie
en: As a result, this greatly increases the possibility of their presence at the same restaurant.

speaker: Dan
en: Great.

speaker: Dan
en: I've learned a lot from your research.

speaker: Dan
en: I think it's meaningful and innovative.

speaker: Dan
en: Thanks for telling me, Jeannie.

speaker: Jeannie
en: You are welcome, Dan.
